overview: A classic Israeli song describing the creation of the world (Genesis). It portrays the beauty of nature—sky, sea, sun, and fruit—as divine gifts given to humanity to guard and cherish.

A dialogue between a person and God about the cycle of sin and repentance. The singer admits to running away and making excuses, expressing a deep desire to finally do God's will sincerely, 'once and for all'.
A powerful song about finding God in everything—both the beautiful and the difficult. Mooki sees God in love, hate, uncertainty, and loneliness, expressing a lack of fear because God is within him.
Based on Psalm 137, this is the eternal vow of the Jewish people never to forget Jerusalem. It is sung at weddings and times of remembrance, pledging that Jerusalem remains the 'head of our joy'.
A song about a broken heart that feels like a stormy sea. Ribo expresses that only God can heal this pain, turn mourning into dancing ('convert my eulogy to forgiveness'), and purify the mundane.

title_english: Hine Yamim Baim (Here Come the Days)
overview: Based on the prophecy of Amos, this song speaks of a spiritual hunger. It says days are coming when people will not be hungry for bread or water, but for the word of God and truth.

A central text from the Passover Haggadah. It reminds us that in every generation, enemies rise against the Jewish people, but God saves them from their hands. Razel's melody made it a modern classic.
A soulful ballad about wandering in Jerusalem and finding the Gate of Mercy. It describes a simple man seeking meaning and redemption, declaring that life ultimately has a purpose.
A deeply emotional prayer song where the singer calls out 'Hear O Israel' to God in a moment of distress ('when the heart cries'), asking for strength and believing that only God can help.
A pop anthem based on the morning prayer of gratitude. It expresses thanks for the new day, for sanity, and for the ability to smile, acknowledging that everything belongs to God.
A deeply spiritual cry asking who truly understands pain and who can heal a broken heart. It reflects a longing for God's embrace and a promise that one will not surrender to despair.

title_english: Lecha Eli Teshukati (To You My God is My Desire)
overview: A famous liturgical poem (Piyut) written by Abraham Ibn Ezra. Meir Banai composed this moving melody for it. It expresses the soul's total devotion and desperate longing for God, pledging body and spirit to Him.

An uplifting anthem about Teshuva (repentance/return). It reassures the listener that it's never too late to change, that 'the train to the neighborhood' is always running, and that God (the Father) is always waiting to help us return.

title_english: Ma Ata Rotze Mimeni (What Do You Want From Me)
overview: A raw conversation with God where Hanan asks for protection from his own ego ('guard me from myself'). He complains about 'empty prayers' and demands a real, tangible connection with God—a hug, a look in the eyes—asking God to fulfill the promise of being the light in the darkness.

Unlike the standard prayer, this is Meir Ariel's personal, poetic interpretation. He thanks God for everything—the good, the bad, and the truth—describing a serene morning scene where the future approaches softly while his loved one smiles in her sleep.

title_english: Mashgiach Min HaChalonot (Watching from the Windows)
overview: Based on Song of Songs, this song emphasizes God's constant, hidden presence. Even when He seems to hide His face ('Mastir Panim'), He is still peeking through the cracks, watching over us, and planning our redemption.

A masterpiece recreating the Yom Kippur service in the Holy Temple. It follows the High Priest (Kohen Gadol) as he purifies himself, enters the Holy of Holies, and counts the prayers, while the people bow in awe at the mention of God's holy name.

A modern song weaving together stories of famous righteous figures (Tzadikkim) like the Tzemach Tzedek, Rabbi Shimon Bar Yochai, and Rabbi Akiva. It connects their struggles and faith to modern life, asking for strength and a lack of fear.
A reflective poem about shared memory, Sinai, resilience, and renewal, written as a devotional meditation of faith and friendship.
A heartfelt prayer song asking God to guard us with compassion, grant light and youthful joy, and keep hope alive in difficult times.

title_english: Kol Akava Letova (Every Delay Is for the Good)
overview: An uplifting song of emunah about accepting life with love, holding onto hope through uncertainty, and remembering the divine spark within every person.

title_english: Lord, Where Shall I Find Thee?
overview: Written by the preeminent poet of the Spanish Golden Age, this piyut (liturgical poem) was composed for the morning service. It was inspired by the philosophical tension between God's "transcendence" (being far away and infinite) and His "immanence" (being close and personal). HaLevi wrote it to express the seeker's journey: searching for the Divine in the vastness of the cosmos only to discover that God is most present within the human heart. It remains a masterpiece of medieval Jewish Neo-Platonism.

Zelda Schneurson Mishkovsky was a deeply religious Israeli poet from a distinguished Chabad lineage, known for her humble and mystical perspective. "The Guest" was written as a reflection on Bitachon (radical trust). Zelda felt that the modern world was too full of entitlement and ego; she wrote this to reframe the human condition as that of a temporary, grateful guest in the Creator's home. It is about trading anger and demands for a sense of sacred hospitality and wonder at the simple details of life.

artist: Attributed to Reb Azriel David Fastag
overview: While the text is based on Maimonides' 12th Principle of Faith, this specific version became the "Hymn of the Camps" during the Holocaust. Tradition holds that Reb Azriel David Fastag, a Modzitzer Hasid, composed this haunting melody in a cattle car bound for Treblinka in 1942. He wrote it to sustain the spirits of his fellow Jews, asserting that even in the absolute darkness of the Shoah, faith in the eventual redemption remained unshakable. It is now a global symbol of Jewish resilience.

Attributed to the 11th-century Spanish philosopher Solomon ibn Gabirol, this is one of the most widely recognized hymns in Jewish liturgy. It was written to reconcile two seemingly contradictory ideas: the vast, terrifying eternity of God as the "Master of the World" and the intimate, comforting God who is "my living Redeemer." The poem moves from cosmic philosophy - God existing before time - to a deeply personal surrender of the soul, expressing a lack of fear because God is "with me."

This is the most famous "Zionide" ever written. HaLevi composed it while living in Spain, consumed by Kissufim (burning longing) for the Land of Israel. The poem personifies Zion as a long-lost lover or mother, asking if she remembers her children scattered in exile. He wrote it to mobilize the spiritual heart of the Jewish people, eventually following his own words and traveling to the Land. Legend says he was killed by an Arab horseman while reciting these very lines at the gates of Jerusalem.

Written in 1891, this was Bialik's first published poem and it established him as the "National Poet." It was inspired by the Hebrew Enlightenment (Haskalah) and the early Zionist movement. The poem uses a bird returning from the "warm lands" (the Land of Israel) to the poet's cold window in Russia as a messenger. It is an expression of the pain of the Diaspora, asking the bird if the land of the fathers is as beautiful as the ancient texts promise, or if it, too, suffers from hardship.

Written in December 1947, just after the UN Partition Plan, this poem was inspired by a quote from Chaim Weizmann: "A state is not handed to a people on a silver platter." Alterman wrote it to honor the young men and women of the Palmach and Haganah who were fighting for independence. It depicts a nation waiting for a miracle, only to be met by a boy and a girl exhausted from battle who declare that they are the silver platter. It is the defining poem of Israel's Remembrance Day.

Commissioned for the 1967 Israel Song Festival just weeks before the Six-Day War, Shemer wrote this out of a deep sense of longing for the Old City, which was then under Jordanian control and inaccessible to Jews. The original verses describe the empty marketplaces and the silent Temple Mount. After the war and the reunification of Jerusalem, Shemer added a final triumphant verse ("We have returned to the cisterns"). It has since become a secondary national anthem, bridging ancient biblical imagery with modern history.

Written for Parashat Mishpatim from the bride's perspective: the Jewish people in exile, faithful to the covenant, longing to return home. Dedicated to Shmuel Yosef Agnon, Zohar Argov, and to parents, spouses, and future loved ones.
Companion poem to "The Palm Bride," voiced from the groom's perspective, expressing longing for the covenant to be fully realized.
